title: Almond and cranberry biscotti
subtitle: Twice-baked Italian biscuits, very crisp
excerpt: Twenty-four biscotti made with toasted almonds and dried cranberries, baked twice: once as a log, then sliced and dried in the oven until hard through. They keep in a tin for three weeks and improve with time. Designed to be dipped in coffee or vin santo.
sourceNotes: Biscotti (twice-baked biscuits) are documented in Italian cookery from the medieval period. The Tuscan almond variety (cantucci di Prato) appears in pre-1928 Italian regional cookery texts and in Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, public domain), which gives the method and proportions. Cross-referenced with pre-1928 Italian household baking literature available through Italian national library digital archives.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily; bake two logs side by side on one tray.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 3 weeks. The flavour improves after a few days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, both bakes. Conventional 180°C. First bake: 25 to 30 minutes until firm. Second bake: 12 to 15 minutes until completely dry.

glossaryTerms.definition: Biscotti takes its name from the Italian for twice-cooked (bis-cotto). The dough is first baked as a whole log, allowed to cool slightly, then sliced on the diagonal and the slices returned to the oven cut-side down for a second bake that dries them completely. The result is a biscuit with almost no moisture: it will not go stale in the usual sense, and it has enough hardness to be dipped in coffee or wine without immediately dissolving. The second bake time determines the final texture: 10 minutes gives a hard exterior with a slightly yielding centre; 15 minutes produces a biscuit that is hard through.

content.text: : first as a log, then as slices laid flat. The double bake drives out all moisture, producing a biscuit that is hard, keeps for weeks, and is designed to be dipped rather than eaten dry. Twenty-four biscotti with toasted almonds and dried cranberries.
Mix and form into a log

Heat the oven to 170°C fan (180°C conventional). Line a baking tray with paper. Beat the eggs, sugar, and vanilla together until pale, about 2 minutes. Sift in the flour, baking powder, and salt and fold until a stiff dough forms. Fold in the almonds, cranberries, and orange zest if using. The dough will be sticky. With damp hands, shape it into a log about 30 cm long and 6 cm wide on the lined tray.

Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until pale golden and firm to the touch. Remove from the oven and leave to cool on the tray for 15 minutes; it will be too crumbly to cut while hot. Using a sharp serrated knife, cut the log on the diagonal into slices about 1 to 1.5 cm thick. Lay the slices cut-side down on the tray.

Return to the oven at 170°C fan for 12 to 15 minutes, until the biscotti are completely dry and pale golden on both sides. They will harden further as they cool. Transfer to a wire rack and leave until cold before storing.
